title: Deputy lieutenant

infobox.value: is a Crown appointment and one of several deputies to the [lord-lieutenant](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant") of a [lieutenancy area](/wiki/Lieutenancy_area "Lieutenancy area") – an English [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England"), Welsh [preserved county](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales"), Scottish [lieutenancy area](/wiki/Lieutenancy_areas_of_Scotland "Lieutenancy areas of Scotland"), or Northern Irish [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") or [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland"). Before the creation of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), all Irish counties had deputy lieutenants.
may be added after a deputy lieutenant's surname and other postnominals – *e.g.*, "John Brown, CBE, DL". The position of Deputy Lieutenant is honorary and receives no remuneration.
The Scotland Act 1998 (Transfer of Functions to the Scottish Ministers etc.) Order 1999 (SI 1999/1820).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Crown appointment in the UK

Not to be confused with [second lieutenant](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant") or [junior lieutenant](/wiki/Junior_lieutenant "Junior lieutenant").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Harry_William_Verelst_%281846%E2%80%931918%29.jpg/250px-Harry_William_Verelst_%281846%E2%80%931918%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harry_William_Verelst_(1846%E2%80%931918).jpg)

[Harry Verelst](/wiki/Harry_Verelst_(cricketer) "Harry Verelst (cricketer)") in the uniform of a deputy lieutenant for the [West Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire") in the early 20th century

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), a **deputy lieutenant** is a Crown appointment and one of several deputies to the [lord-lieutenant](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant") of a [lieutenancy area](/wiki/Lieutenancy_area "Lieutenancy area") – an English [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England"), Welsh [preserved county](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales"), Scottish [lieutenancy area](/wiki/Lieutenancy_areas_of_Scotland "Lieutenancy areas of Scotland"), or Northern Irish [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") or [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland"). Before the creation of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), all Irish counties had deputy lieutenants.

In [formal style](/wiki/Style_(form_of_address) "Style (form of address)"), the postnominal letters **DL** may be added after a deputy lieutenant's surname and other postnominals – *e.g.*, "John Brown, CBE, DL". The position of Deputy Lieutenant is honorary and receives no remuneration.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_lieutenant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Deputy lieutenants are nominated by a lord-lieutenant to assist with their duties (see the [Lieutenancies Act 1997](/wiki/Lieutenancies_Act_1997 "Lieutenancies Act 1997")). Deputy lieutenants receive their commission of appointment via the appropriate government minister by command of the [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch").[[1]](#cite_note-1) In England and Wales, since November 2001, the minister responsible for most appointments is the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), with exceptions such as the [Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Duchy_of_Lancaster "Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In Scotland, since July 1999, it has been the [Scottish Ministers](/wiki/Scottish_Ministers "Scottish Ministers").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Decades ago, a county could have as few as three deputy lieutenants. Today, there may be more than a dozen, as their number is proportional to the county's population. Deputy lieutenants tend to be people who either have served the local community, or have a history of public service in other fields.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Deputy lieutenants represent the lord-lieutenant in his or her absence, including at local ceremonies and official events, from opening exhibitions to inductions of vicars (as requested by the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")). They must live within their lieutenancy area, or within seven miles (11 km) of its boundary.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Their appointments do not terminate with any change of lord-lieutenant,[[6]](#cite_note-6) but they are legally required to retire at age 75.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/John_Samuel_Wilson_%28crop_2%29.jpg/250px-John_Samuel_Wilson_%28crop_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Samuel_Wilson_(crop_2).jpg)

Colonel John S. Wilson, OBE, DL, [Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Derbyshire](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Derbyshire "Lord Lieutenant of Derbyshire"), wearing [his uniform](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant#Uniform "Lord-lieutenant") in 2020

One of the serving deputy lieutenants is appointed to be vice lord-lieutenant,[[7]](#cite_note-7) who in most circumstances will stand in for a lord-lieutenant who cannot be present. The appointment as vice lord-lieutenant usually expires upon the retirement of the lord-lieutenant who made the choice. Generally, the vice lord-lieutenant would then revert to being a regular deputy lieutenant.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Unlike the office of lord-lieutenant, which is an appointment in the gift of the Sovereign, the position of deputy lieutenant is an appointment of the Sovereign's appointee, and therefore not a direct appointment of the Sovereign *per se*.

Deputy lieutenant commissions are published by the clerk of the lieutenancy, as a State Appointment, in either the *[London Gazette](/wiki/London_Gazette "London Gazette")* or the *[Edinburgh Gazette](/wiki/Edinburgh_Gazette "Edinburgh Gazette")*, as appropriate, with the names of the persons appointed deputy lieutenants for that county or area, and the dates of their commissions.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
